1: bārekî nafshî ʾet-YHWH — bless the LORD, O my soul. The unusual self-address: the Psalmist commands his own nefesh (soul, the animated self in its entirety) to bless (bārak: to kneel before, to honor, to pronounce as blessed) YHWH. The soul must be commanded because blessing can be withheld by inertia or distraction — it requires a deliberate act of will. wĕkhol-qĕrābay ʾet-shēm qodshô — let every part of me bless his holy name! Qĕrāb (inner parts, innermost being — the collective interior of the person) to bless the divine name. Not a vague generality but a claim about the totality of inner life.
2: bārekî nafshî ʾet-YHWH wĕʾal-tishkĕḥî kol-gĕmûlāyw — bless the LORD, O my soul, and forget not a single good thing he has done. The repeated self-command (bārekî nafshî) now joined with its corrective: wĕʾal-tishkĕḥî (and do not forget). Forgetting the divine benefits is the besetting failure of covenant ingratitude (Deuteronomy 8:11-14; Psalms 78:11). Gĕmûl (benefit, good done — from gāmal: to ripen, to deal fully with; the full, complete good that YHWH has dealt). The psalm's thanksgiving arises from deliberate remembrance.
3: hassōlēaḥ lĕkhol-ʿăwōnekhî — who forgives every one of your sins. Sālaḥ (to forgive — the divine forgiveness; used only of God in the OT, never of human forgiveness of each other). The participle (hassōlēaḥ: the one who is forgiving) indicates ongoing divine activity, not a single past event. hārōphēʾ lĕkhol-taḥălûʾêkhî — who heals every one of your illnesses. Rāphāʾ (to heal — divine healing parallel to divine forgiveness). The pairing of sin-forgiveness and disease-healing is not a mechanical equation (sin causes all illness) but a theological statement: YHWH addresses the whole damaged human condition.
4: haggōʾēl mishshaḥat ḥayyayikh — who redeems your life from the pit. Gāʾal (to redeem — the kinsman-redeemer's action, restoring family members from danger, debt, or death). Shaḥat (the pit/grave — Sheol's concrete image). hameʿaṭṭĕrêkhî ḥesed wĕraḥămîm — who crowns you with steadfast love and compassion. ʿĀṭar (piel: to crown — the placing of a crown on the head). The attributes themselves become the crown: ḥesed (steadfast love) + raḥămîm (compassion — the plural from reḥem: womb; the deep visceral compassion of a parent for a child). The redeemed person wears divine love and compassion as a royal crown.
5: hammaśbîaʿ bĕṭôb ʿedyēkh — who satisfies you with good so that your strength is renewed. Śābaʿ (to satisfy, fill to fullness — the opposite of the emptiness of sin and disease). ʿAdî (ornament, adornment — what is beautiful; the soul's beauty and dignity restored by divine filling). titḥaddēsh kĕnnesher nĕʿûrāyikh — your youth is renewed like the eagle. Nāshar (eagle or vulture — the large raptor known for apparent renewal; possibly the molting of old feathers for new ones, or the long life of the eagle: in ancient tradition the eagle was thought to renew its youth). Nĕʿûrîm (youth, youthfulness). The renewal of youth as the culmination of the participial string: forgiving, healing, redeeming, crowning, satisfying — all together constitute the restoration of the whole person.
6: ʿōśeh ṣĕdāqôt YHWH ûmishpāṭîm lĕkhol-ʿăshûqîm — the LORD acts with righteousness and justice for all who are oppressed. Ṣĕdāqôt (righteous acts — the plural indicating multiple acts of justice). ʿĀshûq (oppressed — passive participle of ʿāshaq: to squeeze/oppress, those crushed by injustice). The movement from the personal (vv1-5: benefits for the individual soul) to the social (v6: justice for the oppressed). YHWH's ḥesed expressed in both personal healing and social justice.
7: yôdîaʿ dĕrākhāyw lĕmōsheh libnê yisrāʾēl ʿalîlôtāyw — he revealed his ways to Moses and his mighty deeds to the people of Israel. Dārakh (ways — the characteristic patterns of divine action) revealed to Moses specifically — the Sinai self-revelation of Exod 33-34, where Moses asked to know God's ways (hôdîʿênî nā ʾet-dĕrākekhā, Exodus 33:13). ʿAlîlāh (mighty deed, act — the recorded powerful acts) to the whole people. Moses received the why (the ways); Israel received the what (the acts).
8: raḥûm wĕḥannûn YHWH ʾerek ʾappayim wĕrab-ḥāsed — the LORD is compassionate and gracious, patient in anger and overflowing with steadfast love. The direct citation of the Mosaic self-revelation formula of Exodus 34:6 (the middôt — divine attributes). This is the text Moses received when he asked to know God's ways (v7). The four attributes: raḥûm (compassionate — from reḥem: womb), ḥannûn (gracious — giving grace freely), ʾerek ʾappayim (long-nostrilled: slow to anger — the nostril-flaring of rage restrained), rab-ḥesed (great in steadfast love). The most cited divine self-description in the OT (Numbers 14:18; Nehemiah 9:17; Psalms 86:15; Psalms 145:8; Joel 2:13; Jonah 4:2).
9: lōʾ-lāneṣaḥ yārîb wĕlōʾ lĕʿôlām yiṭṭôr — he does not bring charges forever, nor does he hold on to his anger without end. Rîb (to contend, bring a legal charge — the covenant-lawsuit verb; cf. Ps 50's divine lawsuit). Nāṭar (to keep, maintain — to hold a grudge permanently). The temporally bounded nature of divine anger: lāneṣaḥ (forever) and lĕʿôlām (to the age) both negated. YHWH's anger is real but not eternal — the divine patience eventually resolves in forgiveness, not in permanent prosecution.
10: lōʾ khĕḥăṭāʾênû ʿāśāh lānû wĕlōʾ khăʿăwōnōtênû gāmal ʿālênû — he has not treated us as our sins deserve, nor paid us back measure for measure for our iniquities. The double negation: lōʾ… lōʾ — neither sins-proportional treatment (ḥăṭāʾênû: our sins) nor iniquities-proportional repayment (ʿăwōnōtênû gāmal: our iniquities measured back). Gāmal (to deal fully with, to recompense exactly) — the exact recompense that divine mercy withholds. The psalm does not claim there is no divine judgment but that divine mercy prevents full judicial recompense.
11: kî kigbōaḥ shāmayim ʿal-hāʾāreṣ gābar ḥasdô ʿal-yĕrēʾāyw — for as high as the heavens are above the earth, so great is his steadfast love for those who fear him. The spatial metaphor for the immeasurable: the height of the heavens above the earth is the maximum visible distance (it cannot be measured from below). Gābar (to be mighty, prevail — the divine ḥesed is mighty, prevailing in measure). The comparison with the immeasurable does not quantify the ḥesed — it declares it beyond quantification.
12: kirhōq mizrāḥ mimmāʿărab hirḥîq mimmennû ʾet-pĕshāʿênû — as far as east is from west, that is how far he has removed our transgressions from us. The east-west axis is directional, not circular: north and south eventually meet (one could travel far enough north to arrive in the south), but east and west never meet — one can travel east forever without arriving west. The infinite distance in the east-west direction is the image for the complete removal of transgressions (pĕshāʿîm: willful rebellious acts). Hirḥîq (hiphil: to remove to a distance — to place far away).
13: kĕraḥēm ʾāb ʿal-bānîm rāḥam YHWH ʿal-yĕrēʾāyw — just as a father is tender toward his children, so the LORD is tender toward those who fear him. Rāḥam (piel: to show deep compassion — from reḥem: the womb). The father's compassion for his children as the analogy for divine compassion — not the womb-compassion of a mother (though that imagery also appears in Isaiah 49:15) but the protective, tender compassion of a father. The familial metaphor for divine-human relationship grounding the forgiveness of vv9-12.
14: kî-hûʾ yādaʿ yiṣrênû zākhûr kî-ʿāphār ʾănaḥnû — for he knows exactly what we are made of; he has not forgotten that we are dust. Yāṣar (to form, shape — the potter's word; YHWH knows our formation because he formed us, Genesis 2:7). Zākhûr (a passive participle: he is one who remembers — the ongoing divine awareness). ʿĀphār (dust — from Genesis 2:7: wayyîṣer YHWH ʾĕlōhîm ʾet-hāʾādām ʿāphār min-hāʾădāmāh; and Genesis 3:19: ʿāphār ʾattāh wĕʾel-ʿāphār tāshûb). The divine compassion (v13) grounded in the divine knowledge of human frailty: God's mercy is not naïve but precisely calibrated to what we actually are.
15: ʾĕnôsh kĕḥāṣîr yāmāyw kĕṣîṣ haśśādeh kēn yāṣîṣ — as for mortals, their days are like grass; they bloom like a wildflower of the field. ʾĔnôsh (mortal man — the word emphasizing human frailty, from a root meaning to be sick/weak; contrast with ʾādām or ʾîsh). Ḥāṣîr (grass — cf. Psalms 90:5-6; Isaiah 40:6-8). Ṣîṣ haśśādeh (wildflower of the field — the seasonal flower of the Palestinian landscape, beautiful and brief). The grass and wildflower together represent beauty + transience.
16: kî rûaḥ ʿābĕrāh-bô wĕʾênennû — the wind sweeps over it and it vanishes. Rûaḥ (wind — here the hot sirocco that desiccates vegetation rapidly). ʿĀbar (to pass over — the wind's passage is enough to kill the grass). wĕlōʾ-yakkîrennû ʿôd mĕqômô — and the place where it stood no longer knows it. The personification of māqôm (place): the place no longer recognizes the person who stood there. The completeness of mortality — not just the person disappears but their location forgets them. Compare with the permanence of divine ḥesed in the next verse.
17: wĕḥesed YHWH mēʿôlām wĕʿad-ʿôlām ʿal-yĕrēʾāyw — but the steadfast love of the LORD is from age to age over those who fear him. The pivot: the brevity of human life (vv15-16) set against the eternal divine ḥesed. Mēʿôlām wĕʿad-ʿôlām (from age to age — from everlasting to everlasting): the temporal scope of ḥesed is the exact opposite of human transience. wĕṣidqātô libnê bānîm — and his righteousness reaches to their grandchildren. The divine righteousness reaching to generations (bĕnê bānîm: sons of sons/grandchildren) — the covenantal faithfulness that outlasts individual lives and passes through family lines.
18: lĕshōmĕrê bĕrîtô ûlĕzōkhĕrê phiqquddāyw laʿăśôtām — to those who hold to his covenant and keep his commands in mind to live by them. The two conditions defining those who receive the eternal ḥesed: shāmar bĕrît (keeping the covenant — the active maintenance of covenant relationship) + zākar phiqqûdîm laʿăśôt (remembering commands in order to do them — the doing as the purpose of the remembering). The ethical and relational dimensions together: covenant identity + obedient action.
19: YHWH bashshāmayim hēkhîn kissĕʾô ûmalkûtô bakkōl māshālāh — the LORD has set up his throne in the heavens; his kingdom governs everything that exists. Kûn (hiphil perfect: he has established firmly — the divine throne is already established, not being established). Malkût (kingdom, sovereignty) governing bakkōl (over everything — the universal scope). The theological climax: before calling on the cosmic community to praise (vv20-22), the basis is stated — YHWH's universal sovereignty. Every created entity called to bless is within his governance.
20: bārekû YHWH malʾākhāyw gibbōrê kōaḥ — bless the LORD, you his angels — his mighty warriors. The outward expansion of blessing: from the single soul (nafshî, vv1-2) now to the angels (malʾākhîm: messengers, divine council members). Gibbōrê kōaḥ (mighty in strength — the angels as powerful beings, cf. Psalms 29:1's bĕnê ʾēlîm). ʿōśê dĕbārô lishmoaʿ bĕqôl dĕbārô — who carry out his commands, swift to obey his voice. ʿĀśāh dābār (to carry out his word/command) — obedience as the defining activity of the angels. Lishmoaʿ bĕqôl dĕbārô (to hear/obey the voice of his word) — hearing as the prelude to obedience.
21: bārekû YHWH kol-ṣĕbāʾāyw mĕshārĕtāyw ʿōśê rĕṣônô — bless the LORD, all his armies — all who serve him and carry out his purposes! Ṣĕbāʾîm (armies, hosts — the divine armies of heaven, giving YHWH his title ʾĕlōhê ṣĕbāʾôt: God of hosts). Mĕshārĕtāyw (his servants/ministers — those who serve in the divine court). Rāṣôn (his pleasure, will, purpose) done: the angels carry out divine purposes rather than their own. The second circle of blessing: the angelic armies.
22: bārekû YHWH kol-maʿăśāyw bĕkhol-mĕqōmôt memshaltô — bless the LORD, everything he has made, in every place his reign extends. The third circle: all of creation (kol-maʿăśāyw: all his works) in every place his dominion extends. The universal blessing of the Creator by all his creatures — the creation-wide doxology. bārekî nafshî ʾet-YHWH — bless the LORD, O my soul! The return to v1: the psalm that expanded from one soul to all creation returns to the single soul. The cosmic praise collapses back into the individual's personal blessing — showing that the single soul's praise participates in and echoes the cosmic praise of all creation.
